He chased them beyond the passes until, desperate, they threw away arms and ran naked. He took more than three thousand heads and several thousand horses.

sentences.zh: 自是後鮮卑震怖，畏肜不敢復窺塞。

translations.literal: From this after Xianbei shocked afraid, feared Rong not dared again spy passes.
translations.idiomatic: After that the Xianbei were terrified of Zhai Rong and dared not approach the passes.

sentences.zh: 肜以三虜連和，卒為邊害[44]，二十五年，乃使招呼鮮卑，示以財利。

translations.literal: Rong because three caitiffs linked allied, eventually became border harm [44], twenty-fifth year, then sent summon Xianbei, showed them wealth profit.
translations.idiomatic: Because the three barbarian powers in league would be a lasting border threat [44], in the twenty-fifth year Zhai Rong summoned the Xianbei and tempted them with profit.

sentences.zh: 其大都護偏何[45]遣使奉獻，願得歸化，肜慰納賞賜，稍復親附。

translations.literal: Their great chief protector Pian He [45] dispatched envoys offer tribute, wished obtain submit transform, Rong comforted accepted rewarded, gradually again attached close.
translations.idiomatic: Their great chief protector, Pian He [45], sent envoys with tribute and offered submission; Zhai Rong welcomed them, rewarded them, and gradually drew them close again.

translations.literal: 745 page.
translations.idiomatic: Source edition, page 745.

sentences.zh: 其異種滿離、高句驪之屬，遂駱驛款塞，上貂裘好馬，帝輒倍其賞賜。

translations.literal: Their different tribes Manli, Koguryo types, thereupon continuously opened passes, offered sable fur fine horses, emperor always doubled their rewards.
translations.idiomatic: Other peoples such as the Manli and Koguryo then came in steady streams to the passes with sable and fine horses, and the emperor always doubled their rewards.

sentences.zh: 其後偏何邑落諸豪並歸義，願自效。

translations.literal: Afterward Pian He settlement various chiefs together submitted righteousness, wished self serve.
translations.idiomatic: Later the chiefs of Pian He's settlements all submitted and offered their service.

sentences.zh: 肜曰：「審欲立功，當歸擊匈奴，斬送頭首乃信耳。

translations.literal: Rong said: "If truly desire establish merit, ought return strike Xiongnu, behead deliver heads then believed ear.
translations.idiomatic: Zhai Rong said, 'If you truly want merit, go back and strike the Xiongnu and bring me heads—then I will believe you.'

sentences.zh: 」偏何等皆仰天指心曰：「必自效！

translations.literal: " Pian He and others all looked up heaven pointed heart said: "Must self serve!
translations.idiomatic: Pian He and his men looked to heaven, laid hand on heart, and cried, 'We will serve you!'

sentences.zh: 」即擊匈奴左伊*

translations.literal: " Immediately struck Xiongnu left Yiwei-rank Zi department [46], beheaded heads more than two thousand grades, carried heads went commandery.
translations.idiomatic: They thereupon struck the Xiongnu left Yiwei-rank Zi division [46], took more than two thousand heads, and brought the heads to the commandery seat.

translations.literal: (variant graph for title character)
translations.idiomatic: (The manuscript gives a variant graph for the second syllable of the Xiongnu title.)

sentences.zh: **[秩]*訾部[46]，斬首二千餘級，持頭詣郡。

translations.literal: **[rank]* Zi department [46], beheaded heads more than two thousand grades, carried heads went commandery.
translations.idiomatic: The Yiwei-rank Zi division [46] lost more than two thousand men; they brought the heads to the commandery seat.

sentences.zh: 其後歲歲相攻，輒送首級受賞賜。

translations.literal: Afterward year year mutually attacked, always sent head grades received rewards.
translations.idiomatic: Year after year they fought one another and regularly sent in heads for their rewards.

sentences.zh: 自是匈奴衰弱，邊無寇警，鮮卑、烏桓並入朝貢。

translations.literal: From this Xiongnu declined weak, border without raid alarm, Xianbei, Wuhuan together entered court tribute.
translations.idiomatic: The Xiongnu thereafter waned, the border knew no alarms, and Xianbei and Wuhuan alike came to court with tribute.

sentences.zh: 肜為人質厚重毅，體貌絕觿。

translations.literal: Rong as man substance heavy thick firm, body appearance outstanding masses.
translations.idiomatic: Zhai Rong was stolid and resolute, with a striking presence among men.

sentences.zh: 撫夷狄以恩信，皆畏而愛之，故得其死力。

translations.literal: Comforted Yi Di by grace trust, all feared yet loved him, therefore obtained their dead strength.
translations.idiomatic: He ruled the Yi and Di with grace and good faith; they feared and loved him and so gave him their utmost strength.

sentences.zh: 初，赤山烏桓數犯上谷，為邊害，詔書設購賞，*

translations.literal: At first, Chishan Wuhuan several times violated Shanggu, as border harm, edict set bounty reward, *
translations.idiomatic: At first the Chishan Wuhuan had repeatedly raided Shanggu and plagued the frontier; an edict offered a bounty, *

translations.literal: (variant: merit)
translations.idiomatic: (textual variant: merit)

translations.literal: **[severely]* blamed commanderies [47], not able prohibit.
translations.idiomatic: and sternly rebuked the commanderies [47] for failing to stop them.

sentences.zh: 肜乃率勵偏何，遣往討之。

translations.literal: Rong then led encouraged Pian He, dispatched go punish them.
translations.idiomatic: Zhai Rong then roused Pian He and sent him to punish them.

sentences.zh: 永平元年，偏何擊破赤山，斬其魁帥，持首詣肜，塞外震讋[48]。

translations.literal: Yongping first year, Pian He broke Red Mountain, beheaded their chieftain, carried head went Rong, beyond passes shocked awed [48].
translations.idiomatic: In the first year of Yongping, Pian He crushed the Red Mountain band, beheaded their chief, and brought the head to Zhai Rong; beyond the passes all trembled [48].

sentences.zh: 肜之威聲，暢於北方，西自武威，東盡玄菟及樂浪，胡夷皆來內附，野無風塵。

translations.literal: Rong's awe sound, flowed north region, west from Wuwei, east exhaust Xuandu and Lelang, Hu Yi all came inner attach, wilds without wind dust.
translations.idiomatic: Zhai Rong's name carried through the north from Wuwei in the west to Xuandu and Lelang in the east; Hu and Yi came in to submit and the steppe knew no dust of war.

sentences.zh: 乃悉罷緣邊屯兵。

translations.literal: Then fully dismissed along-border garrison troops.
translations.idiomatic: The court was then able to dismiss the frontier garrisons.

sentences.zh: 746頁及之後

translations.literal: 746 page and following.
translations.idiomatic: Source edition, page 746 and following.

sentences.zh: 十二年，徵為太僕。

translations.literal: Twelfth year, summoned as grand coachman.
translations.idiomatic: In the twelfth year he was summoned to the post of grand coachman.

sentences.zh: 肜在遼東幾三十年，衣無兼副。

translations.literal: Rong at Liaodong nearly thirty years, clothes no double set.
translations.idiomatic: He had been in Liaodong nearly thirty years and still had no second suit of clothes.

sentences.zh: 顯宗既嘉其功，又美肜清約，拜日，賜錢百萬，馬三匹，衣被刀□下至居室什物，大小無不悉備。

translations.literal: Emperor Ming both praised his merit, also admired Rong clear restrained, on appointment day, bestowed cash million, horses three, clothes quilts knife lacuna down to dwelling room misc goods, large small none not all prepared.
translations.idiomatic: Emperor Ming admired both his service and his austerity; on the day of his promotion he gave him a million cash, three horses, clothes, quilts, a knife (character missing in text), and every need of his household down to the smallest utensil.

sentences.zh: 帝每見肜，常歎息以為可屬以重任。

translations.literal: Emperor whenever saw Rong, always sighed thought could entrust heavy responsibility.
translations.idiomatic: Whenever the emperor saw Zhai Rong he would sigh that here was a man fit for the heaviest charge.

sentences.zh: 後從東巡狩，過魯，坐孔子講堂，顧指子路室謂左右曰：「此太僕之室。

translations.literal: After followed east hunt tour, passed Lu, sat Confucius lecture hall, looked pointed Zilu's chamber told left right saying: "This grand coachman's chamber.
translations.idiomatic: Later, on an eastern tour, he passed through Lu, sat in Confucius's lecture hall, and pointing to the chamber once assigned to Zilu said to those beside him, 'That was the grand coachman's room.'

sentences.zh: 太僕，吾之禦侮也。

translations.literal: Grand coachman, my shield against insult.
translations.idiomatic: The grand coachman is my shield against insult.'

sentences.zh: 」[49]十六年，使肜以太僕將萬餘騎與南單于左賢王信伐北匈奴，期至涿邪山。

translations.literal: " [49] Sixteenth year, ordered Rong with grand coachman lead more than ten thousand horse with south Chanyu left worthy king Xin campaign north Xiongnu, agreed reach Zhuoxie mountain.
translations.idiomatic: [49] In the sixteenth year he sent Zhai Rong as grand coachman with more than ten thousand horsemen and the southern chanyu's left worthy king, Xin, against the northern Xiongnu, with a rendezvous at Mount Zhuoxie.

sentences.zh: 信初有嫌於肜，行出高闕塞九百餘里，得小山，乃妄言以為涿邪山。

translations.literal: Xin at first had grudge against Rong, march exited Gaoque pass more than nine hundred li, obtained small hill, then falsely said thought was Zhuoxie mountain.
translations.idiomatic: Xin had borne a grudge against Zhai Rong; marching more than nine hundred li beyond Gaoque Pass he found a low hill and falsely declared it Mount Zhuoxie.

sentences.zh: 肜到不見虜而還，坐逗留畏懦下獄免。

translations.literal: Rong arrived not saw caitiffs and returned, sat delay timid cowardice down prison dismissed.
translations.idiomatic: Zhai Rong found no enemy and withdrew; for dilatoriness and cowardice he was jailed and stripped of office.

sentences.zh: 肜性沉毅內重，自恨見詐無功，出獄數日，歐血死。

translations.literal: Rong nature sunk firm inner weight, self hated seen deceived no merit, exited prison several days, vomited blood died.
translations.idiomatic: Deeply ashamed of having been tricked into failure, he vomited blood and died a few days after leaving prison.

sentences.zh: 臨終謂其子曰：「吾蒙國厚恩，奉使不稱，微績不立，身死誠慚恨。

translations.literal: Facing end told his son saying: "I received state's thick favor, carried mission not matched, slight merit not established, body death truly shame regret.
translations.idiomatic: At the last he told his son, 'I have received too much from the state; I disgraced my mission and won no merit. My death is true shame and regret.'

sentences.zh: 義不可以無功受賞，死後，若悉簿上所得賜物[50]，身自詣兵屯，效死前行，以副吾心。」

translations.literal: Righteousness not permissible without merit receive reward, after death, if fully register upward obtained bestowed goods [50], body self go army camp, offer death forward march, to match my heart."
translations.idiomatic: Honor does not allow reward without merit. After I die, register every gift the court gave me [50], take yourself to the camp, and die in the vanguard—that would match my heart.'"

sentences.zh: 既卒，其子逢上疏具陳遺言。

translations.literal: Already died, his son Feng submitted memorial fully stated dying words.
translations.idiomatic: When he died, his son Zhai Feng memorialized his last words in full.

sentences.zh: 帝雅重肜，方更任用，聞之大驚，召問逢疾狀，嗟歎者良久焉。

translations.literal: Emperor always esteemed Rong, just about again employ, heard greatly startled, summoned asked Feng illness situation, sighed long time.
translations.idiomatic: The emperor, who had thought highly of Zhai Rong and meant to employ him again, was stunned; he summoned Feng to ask how the illness had gone and sighed a long while.

sentences.zh: 烏桓、鮮卑追思肜無已，每朝賀京師，常過頤拜謁，仰天號泣乃去。

translations.literal: Wuhuan, Xianbei pursued thought Rong without end, every court congratulate capital, always passed tomb pay court visit, looked up heaven wailed wept then departed.
translations.idiomatic: The Wuhuan and Xianbei could not forget Zhai Rong: whenever they came to the capital for court they would detour to his tomb, wail to heaven, and only then leave.

sentences.zh: 遼東吏人為立祠，四時奉祭焉。

translations.literal: Liaodong clerks people for him established shrine, four seasons offer sacrifice.
translations.idiomatic: The officials and people of Liaodong built him a shrine and sacrificed at the four seasons.

sentences.zh: 肜既葬，子參遂詣奉車都尉竇固，從軍擊車師有功，稍遷遼東太守。

translations.literal: Rong already buried, son Shen then went coach commandant Dou Gu, followed army strike Jushi had merit, gradually transferred Liaodong governor.
translations.idiomatic: After Zhai Rong's burial his son Shen joined coach commandant Dou Gu, campaigned against Jushi with distinction, and rose to governor of Liaodong.

sentences.zh: 永元中，鮮卑入郡界，參坐沮敗，下獄死。

translations.literal: Within Yongyuan, Xianbei entered commandery border, Shen sat defeat discouraged, down prison died.
translations.idiomatic: During the Yongyuan era the Xianbei entered the commandery; Shen was charged with defeat, thrown into prison, and died.

sentences.zh: 肜子孫多為邊吏者，皆有名稱。

translations.literal: Rong descendants many who were border clerks, all had fair names.
translations.idiomatic: Many of Zhai Rong's descendants served on the frontier and all won good reputations.

sentences.zh: 論曰：祭肜武節剛方，動用安重，雖條侯、穰苴之倫，不能過也[51]。

translations.literal: Discussion says: Zhai Rong martial integrity rigid square, movements used secure heavy, although Tiao Hou, Rangju types, not able surpass also [51].
translations.idiomatic: The appraiser writes: Zhai Rong's military integrity was stern and steadfast, his conduct grave and sure—even men of the stamp of Marquis Tiao or Sima Rangju could not surpass him [51].

sentences.zh: 且臨守偏海，政移獷俗[52]，徼人請符以立信，胡貊數級於郊下[53]，至乃臥鼓邊亭，滅烽幽障者將三十年。

translations.literal: Moreover faced guarded remote sea, government shifted fierce customs [52], frontier people requested tallies to establish trust, Hu Mo several times presented heads at suburbs below [53], until even drums laid in border pavilions, beacons extinguished in dark passes, nearly thirty years.
translations.idiomatic: He governed a distant seaboard, turned rough frontier ways by policy [52], bound faith with tallies for the tribesmen [52], and piled Hu and Mo heads beneath the walls [53]; for almost thirty years the watchtowers could idle their drums and douse their beacon fires.

sentences.zh: 古所謂「必世而後仁」[54]，豈不然哉！

translations.literal: Ancient so-called "must generation then afterward benevolence" [54], how not so!
translations.idiomatic: This is what the ancients meant by 'benevolence must wait a generation' [54]—and was it not so here!

sentences.zh: 而一眚[55]之故，以致感憤，惜哉，畏法之敝也[56]！

translations.literal: Yet one fault [55]'s reason, to reach moved resentment, alas, fear law's harm also [56]!
translations.idiomatic: Yet for one fault [55] he was driven to such grief—alas, the harm of making law too terrible [56]!

sentences.zh: 贊曰：期啟燕門，霸冰虖河。

translations.literal: Encomium says: Qi opened Yan gate, Ba ice Hutuo river.
translations.idiomatic: The encomium runs: Qi opened the gate of Yan; Ba froze the Hutuo.

sentences.zh: 祭遵好禮，臨戎雅歌。

translations.literal: Ji Zun loved rites, facing arms refined song.
translations.idiomatic: Zhai Zun loved ritual and sang the hymns in camp.

sentences.zh: 肜抗遼左，邊廷懷和。

translations.literal: Rong resisted Liaodong left, border court cherished harmony.
translations.idiomatic: Zhai Rong held the eastern Liaodong march; the court and the steppe met in peace.

sentences.zh: 校勘記

translations.idiomatic: Section heading: collation notes.

translations.idiomatic: Collation note for p. 731 line 4: the Jijie cites Hui Dong that the Eastern View Records reads "followed the pacification of Hebei" instead of the Ji campaign.

translations.idiomatic: Collation note for p. 731 line 12: bracketed "Zao" supplied per Qian Daxin's Jijie commentary.

translations.idiomatic: Collation: the graph should be another character with the grain radical, but all editions read "Zao"; the text is left unchanged.

sentences.zh: 七三二頁二行攝*

translations.idiomatic: Collation on "fight again": the head-wrap graph denotes a horse flank cloth; Qi was struck in the forehead, so the reading should be "ze" (headcloth).

sentences.zh: 王先謙謂東觀記正作「幘」。

translations.idiomatic: Wang Xianqian notes that the Eastern View Records correctly writes "ze".

translations.idiomatic: Collation: the head-wrap graph was corrupted as "indignant" and has been corrected.

sentences.zh: 七三四頁二行復封丹弟統為建平侯按：集解引惠棟說，謂水經注作「平輿」，屬汝南也。

translations.idiomatic: Collation note for p. 734 line 2: Jijie cites Hui Dong that the Shuijing zhu gives "Pingyu" in Runan for the fief.

translations.idiomatic: Collation note for p. 735 line 9: the Dianben examination doubts the place name "Wang township" in the Treatise on Administrative Geography.

sentences.zh: 七三五頁一０行說文曰歋□手相笑也按：集解引孫星衍說，謂說文作「歋愈」，並無「□」字。

translations.idiomatic: Collation note for p. 735 line 10: Jijie cites Sun Xingyan on the Shuowen gloss.

sentences.zh: 云「人相笑相歋愈」，不云「手相笑」。

translations.literal: Says "people mutually laugh mutually ye yu", not say "hand mutually laugh".
translations.idiomatic: The Shuowen defines mutual ridicule among people, not specifically hands.

sentences.zh: 注誤。

translations.literal: Commentary error.
translations.idiomatic: The commentary is judged erroneous.

sentences.zh: 七三五頁一五行死者脫衣以斂之傷者躬親以養之刊誤謂按文脫衣可言「以斂之」，躬親不宜復有「以」字。

translations.literal: Page 735 line 15 dead stripped clothes to bury wounded personally nurture collation Kanwu says stripping clothes may take "to bury them", personally nurture should not repeat "to".
translations.idiomatic: Collation note for p. 735 line 15: Kanwu argues against redundant "to" before "nurture".

sentences.zh: 按：「以斂之」與「以養之」相對成文，劉說泥。

translations.literal: Collation: "to bury" and "to nurture" parallel as phrasing; Liu's objection overly narrow.
translations.idiomatic: Collation: the parallel "to bury" / "to nurture" is idiomatic; Liu's objection is rejected.

sentences.zh: 七三六頁八行茂觿疲勞按：御覽二八四引。

translations.idiomatic: Collation note for p. 736 line 8: cited from Yulan 284.

translations.literal: Below "Mao" has "Jian" graph.
translations.idiomatic: The citation adds the graph "Jian" after "Mao".

translations.literal: Page 737 line 1 garrison *field* Hangu Pass supplemented per Ji and Dian editions.
translations.idiomatic: Collation note for p. 737 line 1: "field" in garrison Hangu supplied from Ji and Dian editions.

sentences.zh: 七三八頁三行溫余水出上谷居庸關東按：「溫余水」當作「□余水」，說詳楊守敬水經註疏。

translations.literal: Page 738 line 3 Warm remainder water exits Shanggu Juyong pass east collation: "Warm remainder" ought be different graph plus remainder, explained in Yang Shoujing's Shuijing zhu shu.
translations.idiomatic: Collation note for p. 738 line 3: the river name as given should be emended per Yang Shoujing's Shuijing commentary.

sentences.zh: 七三九頁四行臧宮等入箕關按：集解引惠棟說，謂東觀記「箕關」作「天中關」。

translations.idiomatic: Collation note for p. 739 line 4: Eastern View Records variant for Ji Pass.

translations.idiomatic: Collation note for p. 739 line 4: geographic gloss on Baigu versus Baihua.

sentences.zh: 「柏華」蓋「柏谷」之誤。

translations.literal: "Baihua" probably "Baigu" error.
translations.idiomatic: "Baihua" is probably a corruption of "Baigu".

sentences.zh: 七三九頁五行時新城蠻中山賊張滿按：集解引惠棟說，謂續志新城有鄤聚，今名蠻中。

translations.literal: Page 739 line 5 at that time Xincheng Man Zhongshan bandit Zhang Man collation: Jijie cites Hui Dong, continuation treatise Xincheng has Man stockade now named Manzhong.
translations.idiomatic: Collation note for p. 739 line 5: identification of Manzhong stockade.

sentences.zh: 說文作「□中」。

translations.idiomatic: Shuowen writes the toponym with a different initial graph.

sentences.zh: 七三九頁八行鄧奉弟終按：集解引惠棟說，謂「終」一作「觿」，古通。

translations.idiomatic: Collation note for p. 739 line 8: variant graphs for the younger brother's name.

translations.idiomatic: Collation note for p. 741 line 14: Kanwu emends "first" to "Guang" (Guangwu).

sentences.zh: 七四二頁08行：不忘俎豆[35]，按：王先謙謂東觀記作「不忘王室」。

translations.literal: Page 742 line 08: not forget sacrificial vessels [35] collation: Wang Xianqian says Eastern View Records reads "not forget royal house".
translations.idiomatic: Collation note for p. 742 line 08: variant wording in Eastern View Records.

translations.idiomatic: Collation fragment p. 742 line 08.

translations.literal: (variant: as)
translations.idiomatic: This line records a textual variant.

translations.idiomatic: Collation note on the graph for Zhai Rong's name [41].

translations.idiomatic: Collation note [43] on "front" in vanguard.

translations.idiomatic: Han guanyi: east and west bureau clerks rank at four hundred dan, other bureau clerks at three hundred dan.

translations.literal: Bandit bureau, in charge bandit affairs.
translations.idiomatic: The bandit bureau handled banditry.

translations.idiomatic: Zhouli: the prison servant manages certain inner-palace duties.

translations.idiomatic: Zheng Zhong glosses: clearing the road for the ruler, as with modern jingbi.

translations.idiomatic: Collation: emendation of the gloss.

translations.literal: Leyang, county name, belonged Changshan commandery.
translations.idiomatic: Leyang was a county in Changshan commandery.

translations.idiomatic: Footnote on Zao place name.

translations.idiomatic: Supplemented per Jijie citing Qian Daxin.

translations.idiomatic: Collation: graph discussion as in parallel note above.

sentences.zh: 肥累，故肥子國也，漢以為縣，故城在今棗城縣西南，並屬真定國。

translations.literal: Feilei, former Fei zi state, Han made county, old city today Zao city county southwest, both belonged Zhending state.
translations.idiomatic: Feilei was the old state of Fei, made a Han county southwest of present Zao city, within Zhending.

sentences.zh: 累，音力追反。

translations.literal: Lei, sound li-zhui fan.
translations.idiomatic: Lei is read with the li-zhui fan spelling.

sentences.zh: 兒音五奚反。

translations.literal: Er sound wu-xi fan.
translations.idiomatic: Er is read with the wu-xi fan spelling.

sentences.zh: 攝猶正也。

translations.literal: She still means correct.
translations.idiomatic: She here means to set right or correct.

sentences.zh: 唯天子得稱警□。

translations.literal: Only Son of Heaven obtains call jing lacuna.
translations.idiomatic: Only the Son of Heaven may use the full clearing-of-the-way terminology (lacuna in text).

sentences.zh: 博平，縣名，屬東郡，在今博州縣也。

translations.literal: Boping, county name, belonged Dong commandery, at today Bo prefecture county.
translations.idiomatic: Boping was a county in Dong commandery, in the area of present-day Bo prefecture.

sentences.zh: 力，苦戰也。

translations.literal: Li: bitter fighting.
translations.idiomatic: The gloss explains *li* as denoting a hard-fought, grueling battle.

sentences.zh: 建平，縣名，屬沛郡，故城在今亳州酇縣西北，一名馬頭城。

translations.literal: Jianping is a county name, subordinate to Pei commandery; its old walled town was northwest of present-day Zui County in Bozhou, also called Matoucheng.
translations.idiomatic: Jianping was a county in Pei commandery; its former seat lay northwest of present-day Zui County in Bozhou, also known as Matou (“Horse Head”) City.

sentences.zh: 葛陵，縣名，故城在汝南，故鮦陽縣也。

translations.literal: Geling is a county name; its old walled town was in Runan; it was the former Tongyang County.
translations.idiomatic: Geling was a county whose ancient seat lay in Runan on the ground of the old Tongyang County.

sentences.zh: 說文曰：「□，臂上也。

translations.literal: The *Shuowen* says: “Gong (肱): the upper part of the arm.”
translations.idiomatic: The *Shuowen* defines the graph *gong*, the standard character for the forearm, as the upper arm.

sentences.zh: 」□音公弘反。

translations.literal: ” The graph is read with the gong-hong fanqie spelling.
translations.idiomatic: The commentary gives its fanqie spelling as gong-hong.

sentences.zh: 續漢書曰：「上幸廣陽城門，設祖道，閱過諸將，以遵新破漁陽，令最在前。」

translations.literal: The *Continued Han History* says: “The sovereign visited the Guangyang City Gate, set up a farewell avenue, reviewed the generals as they passed, and because Zun had newly defeated Yuyang, ordered him to take the foremost place.”
translations.idiomatic: The *Xu Han shu* records that Guangwu went out to the Guangyang gate, laid a ritual send-off lane, and reviewed the generals as they rode by; because Zun had just crushed the enemy at Yuyang, the emperor had him lead the column.

sentences.zh: 解故謂解脫事故，以為辭說。

translations.literal: “Explaining the reasons” means slipping free of blame for misdeeds and turning that into a verbal defense.
translations.idiomatic: *Jie gu* means spinning an exculpatory story—casting off responsibility for what had gone wrong and dressing it up as reasoned explanation.

sentences.zh: 黃門，署名。

translations.literal: Yellow Gates: an office title.
translations.idiomatic: “Yellow Gates” (*huangmen*) was the name of an imperial bureau.

sentences.zh: 前書曰：「是時名倡皆集黃門。

translations.literal: The *Former Han History* says: “At that time famous entertainers all gathered at the Yellow Gates.”
translations.idiomatic: The earlier history notes that celebrated performers were then assembled at the Yellow Gates.

sentences.zh: 」武樂，執干戚以舞也。

translations.literal: ” Martial music: grasping shield and halberd to dance.
translations.idiomatic: “Martial music” meant dancers bearing shield and battle-axe in the military choreography.

sentences.zh: 良猶深也，本或作「久」。

translations.literal: Liang here means “deep”; the original sometimes reads *jiu* (“long-lasting”).
translations.idiomatic: Here *liang* carries the sense “deep”; some manuscripts read *jiu* instead.

sentences.zh: 東觀記曰：「時遵屯汧。

translations.literal: The *Eastern Lodge Record* says: “At that time Zun was stationed at Qian.”
translations.idiomatic: The *Dongguan ji* notes that Zun was then encamped along the Qian River.

sentences.zh: 詔書曰：『將軍連年距難，觿兵即卻，復獨按部，功勞爛然。

translations.literal: The edict said: “General, year after year you have held the line against hardship; though enemy troops press in, you alone still hold your sector—your merit shines clear.
translations.idiomatic: The edict ran: “Year on year you have borne the brunt of the fighting; though arms clash at your front, you alone keep your command in order—your service blazes with distinction.”

sentences.zh: 兵退無宿戒，徹食不豫具，今乃調度，恐力不堪。

translations.literal: When troops withdraw there is no standing alert; when provisions are stripped away they are not prepared in advance; if we now shift dispositions, I fear your strength cannot bear it.
translations.idiomatic: “Your men march off without a standing garrison; when rations are cut there is no stockpile. If we reshuffle deployments now, I worry your command will be stretched past endurance.”

sentences.zh: 國家知將軍不易，亦不遺力。

translations.literal: The state knows the general is not in an easy position, yet it is not stinting its effort either.
translations.idiomatic: “The court knows how hard this is on you, and it is not holding back support.”

sentences.zh: 今送縑千匹，以賜吏士。』」

translations.literal: Now I send a thousand bolts of silk to reward officers and soldiers.””
translations.idiomatic: “Here are a thousand bolts of silk for you to distribute among your officers and men.””

sentences.zh: 「緣」或作「彩」。

translations.literal: “Yuan” is sometimes written “cai.”
translations.idiomatic: One manuscript reads *cai* where others have *yuan*.

sentences.zh: 東觀記曰：「上還幸城門，閱過喪車，瞻望涕泣。」

translations.literal: The *Eastern Lodge Record* says: “When the sovereign returned and visited the gate, he reviewed passing funeral carts, looked up, and wept.”
translations.idiomatic: The *Dongguan ji* adds that on his way back Guangwu halted at the gate, watched the hearses file past, lifted his eyes, and wept.

sentences.zh: 霍光薨，宣帝及上官太后親臨光喪，使太中大夫任宣、侍御史五人持節護喪事。

translations.literal: When Huo Guang died, Emperor Xuan and Empress Dowager Shangguan personally attended his mourning; he sent Grand Counsellor of the Palace Ren Xuan and five attendant censors, each bearing a staff of authority, to supervise the funeral.
translations.idiomatic: At Huo Guang’s death, Emperor Xuan and Empress Dowager Shangguan both went in person to the lying-in-state; the throne dispatched Grand Palace Counsellor Ren Xuan and five attendant censors with credentialed staffs to oversee the obsequies.

sentences.zh: 東觀記曰：「時下宣帝臨霍將軍儀，令公卿讀視，以為故事。」

translations.literal: The *Eastern Lodge Record* says: “At that time the procedure for Emperor Xuan’s attendance at General Huo’s mourning was issued down for the dukes and ministers to read aloud, taking it as precedent.”
translations.idiomatic: The *Dongguan ji* notes that Xuan’s obsequies for General Huo were then circulated in full for the high ministers to review as a model for state mourning.

sentences.zh: 孔子曰：「尊五美，屏四惡。」

translations.literal: Confucius said: “Honor the five beauties; banish the four evils.”
translations.idiomatic: Confucius speaks of “honoring the five beauties and casting out the four evils.”

sentences.zh: 前書曰：「蕭何奏事不名，入門不趨。」

translations.literal: The *Former Han History* says: “When Xiao He presented memorials he did not sign his name; entering the gate he did not quicken his step.”
translations.idiomatic: The earlier annals record that Xiao He could submit memorials without signing his name and enter the palace gate without breaking into the hurried “mincing” gait.

sentences.zh: 疇，等也。

translations.literal: Chou: equals; peers.
translations.idiomatic: Here *chou* means “of the same rank” or “a match.”

sentences.zh: 言功臣死後，子孫襲封，世世與先人等。

translations.literal: It means that after meritorious ministers died, their sons and grandsons inherited fiefs, generation after generation on a par with their forebears.
translations.idiomatic: That is, once a founding minister died, his heirs kept the noble title in unbroken succession, each generation standing equal to the original honoree.

sentences.zh: 前書，高祖與功臣剖符作誓，丹書鐵契，金匱石室，藏之宗廟。

translations.literal: The *Former Han History*: Gaozu with the meritorious ministers split tallies and swore oaths, vermilion text on iron covenant-slips, in golden caskets and stone chambers, stored in the ancestral temple.
translations.idiomatic: The *Han shu* recounts how Gaozu “clove the tallies” with his chief ministers, sealed oaths in vermilion on iron, and lodged those covenants—along with golden casings and stone archives—in the imperial temple.

sentences.zh: 漢興至此二百餘年，言「數百」者，謂以百數之。

translations.literal: From Han’s rise to this point was more than two hundred years; saying “several hundred” means reckoning by hundreds.
translations.idiomatic: The Han house had then stood for over two centuries; speaking of “several hundred” years is a round figure counted by centuries.

sentences.zh: 卓，高也。

translations.literal: Zhuo: high.
translations.idiomatic: Here *zhuo* means “lofty” or “eminent.”

sentences.zh: 前書賈山上書曰：「古之賢君於其臣也，尊其爵祿而親之，疾則臨視之無數，死則往吊哭之，臨其小斂大斂，可謂盡禮也，故臣下竭力盡死以報其上。」

translations.literal: In the *Former Han History* Jia Shan’s memorial says: “The worthy rulers of antiquity toward their ministers honored their ranks and stipends and drew close to them; if they fell ill he would visit without limit; if they died he would go to condole, weep at the laying-out for both minor and major dressing, which can be called exhausting ritual; therefore ministers below exhausted their strength and died to repay their superior.”
translations.idiomatic: Jia Shan’s memorial to Emperor Wen quotes the old ideal: a sage king raised his ministers in rank and salary, stayed close to them, visited them tirelessly when they were ill, mourned them at death, and attended both the minor and major laying-out—ritual carried to the limit—so that those below would spend their last breath repaying him.

sentences.zh: 即隴坻上。

translations.literal: That is, atop the Long Slope.
translations.idiomatic: That is, the Long Slope (*Long di*) divide.

sentences.zh: 磨，兵磨也。

translations.literal: Mo: the grinding of arms.
translations.idiomatic: The gloss takes *mo* as the clash and friction of armed hosts.

sentences.zh: 謂吳漢、耿弇等悉奔還，唯遵獨留不卻。

translations.literal: It means Wu Han, Geng Yan, and the rest all raced back, while Zun alone remained and did not withdraw.
translations.idiomatic: While Wu Han, Geng Yan, and others broke off and rode for safety, Zun alone held his ground and did not pull back.

sentences.zh: 言不侵擾。

translations.literal: It means they did not plunder or harass.
translations.idiomatic: That is, his men did not molest the populace.

sentences.zh: 論語孔子曰：「仁以為己任，不亦重乎。

translations.literal: The *Analects* records Confucius: “To take benevolence as one’s burden—is it not heavy?
translations.idiomatic: Confucius says in the *Analects*: “If you make benevolence your burden, is it not a heavy one?”

sentences.zh: 死而後已，不亦遠乎。」

translations.literal: Only with death does it end—is it not far?”
translations.idiomatic: “You lay it down only at death—is that not a long road?”

sentences.zh: 雅歌謂雅詩也。

translations.literal: “Elegant songs” means the canonical odes.
translations.idiomatic: “Ya songs” refers to the court hymns of the *Book of Odes*.

sentences.zh: 禮記投壺經曰：「壺頸修七寸，腹修五寸，口徑二寸半，容斗五升。

translations.literal: The *Rites of Zhou* pitch-pot manual says: “The pot’s neck is seven inches long, its belly five inches, mouth diameter two and a half inches, holding one *dou* five *sheng*.
translations.idiomatic: The pitch-pot chapter of the *Book of Rites* gives the vessel’s dimensions: a seven-inch neck, a five-inch belly, a two-and-a-half-inch mouth, and a capacity of one *dou* and five *sheng*.

sentences.zh: 壺中實小豆焉，為其矢之躍而出也。

translations.literal: Small beans are placed inside the pot so that the arrows will not bounce out.
translations.idiomatic: They filled the belly with small beans so the arrows would not ricochet out.

sentences.zh: 矢以柘若棘，長二尺八寸，無去其皮，取其堅而重。

translations.literal: Arrows use mulberry or jujube wood, two feet eight inches long; do not strip the bark, taking their hardness and weight.
translations.idiomatic: The arrows were of mulberry or jujube, two feet eight inches long, bark left on for heft and stiffness.

sentences.zh: 投之勝者飲不勝者，以為優劣也。」

translations.literal: The victor in throwing drinks; the loser does not drink, thereby marking superiority and inferiority.”
translations.idiomatic: The winner drank while the loser went dry—an easy way to mark who was up and who was down.”

sentences.zh: ^ 35.0 35.1 按：王先謙謂東觀記作「不忘王室」。

translations.literal: Editorial note: Wang Xianqian argues the *Eastern Lodge Record* originally read “did not forget the royal house.”
translations.idiomatic: Wang Xianqian observes that the *Dongguan ji* wording should be “never forgot the royal house.”

translations.literal: Emended according to the Jie and Dian manuscript editions.
translations.idiomatic: The text follows the Jie and Dian recensions.

sentences.zh: 謚法，周書之篇，周公制焉。

translations.literal: The law of posthumous titles is a chapter in the *Zhou* documents; the Duke of Zhou established it.
translations.idiomatic: The “canons of posthumous names” come from a *Zhou* document traditionally ascribed to the Duke of Zhou.

sentences.zh: 容車，容飾之車，像生時也。

translations.literal: A “appearance carriage” is a carriage with display fittings, imitating how he looked in life.
translations.idiomatic: The *rong* hearse was the ornamented chariot that reproduced the look of the man while alive.

sentences.zh: 介士，甲士也。

translations.literal: Armored soldiers: men in mail.
translations.idiomatic: *Jie shi* means mailed escorts.

sentences.zh: 東觀記曰：「遣校尉發騎士四百人，被玄甲、兜鍪，兵車軍陳送葬。」

translations.literal: The *Eastern Lodge Record* says: “He dispatched a colonel to raise four hundred horsemen clad in black lacquered armor and helmets, arrayed chariots and battle lines, to escort the burial.”
translations.idiomatic: The *Dongguan ji* adds that the court sent a colonel with four hundred mailed riders in black lacquer armor and helmets, drawn up with war wagons in battle order, to convoy the cortège.

sentences.zh: ^ 39.0 39.1 按：集解引沉欽韓說，謂袁紀作「威侯」。

translations.literal: Editorial note: The *Collected Explanations* cites Shen Qinhan to the effect that the *Yuan Ji* reads “Marquis Wei.”
translations.idiomatic: Shen Qinhan, cited in the collected commentary, notes that Fan Ye’s *Han ji* gives the posthumous style as “Marquis Wei.”

sentences.zh: 東觀記曰「上數嗟歎，□尉銚期見上感慟，對曰『陛下至仁，哀念祭遵不已，腢臣各懷慚懼』」也。

translations.literal: The *Eastern Lodge Record* says: “The sovereign sighed again and again; Colonel Yao Qi, seeing how stricken the emperor was, replied: ‘Your Majesty’s utmost benevolence—your grief for Zun will not end—so every minister harbors shame and fear.’”
translations.idiomatic: The *Dongguan ji* quotes Guangwu sighing repeatedly until Colonel Yao Qi, seeing his anguish, said: “Such boundless kindness—your mourning for Zun never slackens—leaves each of us ministers abashed and anxious.”

sentences.zh: ^ 41.0 41.1 按：汲本、殿本「肜」作「彤」，通鑒或作「彤」，或作「肜」。

translations.literal: Editorial note: In the Jie and Dian editions *rong* is written *tong*; the *Comprehensive Mirror* varies between *tong* and *rong*.
translations.idiomatic: Manuscripts disagree on the character for Rong: Jie and Dian favor the graph read *tong* in the sense of cinnabar red, while the *Zizhi tongjian* alternates between that spelling and the form read *rong*.

sentences.zh: 襄賁，縣名，屬東海郡，故城在今沂州臨沂縣南。

translations.literal: Xiangben is a county name, subordinate to Donghai commandery; its old walled town was south of present-day Linyi County in Yi prefecture.
translations.idiomatic: Xiangben County belonged to Donghai; its old seat lay south of modern Linyi in the Yi region.

sentences.zh: 賁音肥。

translations.literal: Ben is read like *fei*.
translations.idiomatic: The second syllable *ben* is pronounced like English “fay” (*fei*).

sentences.zh: ^ 43.0 43.1 御覽三０二引作「常為士卒前鋒」，東觀記作「常為士卒先鋒」，今據御覽補「前」字。

translations.literal: Editorial note: *Imperial Digest* 302 cites “always acted as vanguard for the troops”; the *Eastern Lodge Record* reads “vanguard for the soldiers”; now the word “front” is supplied per the *Digest*.
translations.idiomatic: The *Yulan* quotation has “always led from the front for the troops,” whereas the *Dongguan ji* reads “vanguard for the soldiers”; the extra character *qian* (“ahead”) is restored from the *Yulan* text.

sentences.zh: 卒，終也。

translations.literal: Zu: end; finally.
translations.idiomatic: Here *zu* means “in the end” or “at last.”

sentences.zh: 三虜謂匈奴、鮮卑及赤山烏桓。

translations.literal: The three barbarians means the Xiongnu, the Xianbei, and the Chishan Wuhuan.
translations.idiomatic: The “three barbarian powers” are the Xiongnu, Xianbei, and Wuhuan of Chishan.

sentences.zh: 鮮卑名也。

translations.literal: A Xianbei name.
translations.idiomatic: This is a Xianbei tribal designation.

translations.literal: Editorial note 46.0–46.1: Namely striking the Xiongnu Left Yi—
translations.idiomatic: Scholarly note: the clause should continue “striking the Xiongnu Left Yi—”

translations.literal: (zhi)
translations.idiomatic: The damaged graph is *zhi*, the element meaning rank or order, and it belongs to the Xiongnu title.

sentences.zh: **[秩]*訾部，據集解本改，與前書匈奴傳合。

translations.literal: —zhi Zi department; emended per the *Collected Explanations* edition to match the “Treatise on the Xiongnu” in the *Former Han History*.
translations.idiomatic: —*zhi zi* band; the reading follows the Jijie recension and aligns with the *Han shu* “Xiongnu Account.”

translations.literal: Editorial note 47.0–47.1:
translations.idiomatic: Further editorial note:

translations.literal: (qie)
translations.idiomatic: The graph *qie*, read in the sense of cutting or incisive rebuke, begins the compound *qie ze*, to rebuke harshly.

sentences.zh: **[切]*責州郡，據刊誤改。

translations.literal: —severely rebuke the commanderies and districts; emended per the *Kanwu* list of errors.
translations.idiomatic: —*qie ze* (“sharply rebuke”) the commanderies; corrected from a mistaken graph per the *Kanwu*.

sentences.zh: 音之涉反。

translations.literal: It is read with the zhi-she fanqie spelling.
translations.idiomatic: Fanqie spelling: zhi-she.

sentences.zh: 尚書大傳曰：「孔子曰：『吾有四友焉。

translations.literal: The *Great Tradition of the Documents* says: “Confucius said: ‘I have four friends.
translations.idiomatic: The *Shangshu dazhuan* quotes Confucius: “I count four true friends.”

sentences.zh: 自吾得回也，門人加親，是非胥附邪？

translations.literal: Since I obtained Yan Hui, the disciples have become closer—is this not “clinging attachment”?”
translations.idiomatic: “Once I gained Hui, my students drew nearer to one another—is that not true *xu fu*?”

sentences.zh: 自吾得賜也，遠方之士日至，是非奔走邪？

translations.literal: Since I obtained Ci, gentlemen from distant regions arrive daily—is this not “running to serve”?”
translations.idiomatic: “Once I gained Zigong, men from afar flocked in—is that not true *ben zou*?”

sentences.zh: 自吾得師也，前有光，後有輝，是非先後邪？

translations.literal: Since I obtained Shi, there is light in front and radiance behind—is this not “before and after”?”
translations.idiomatic: “Once I gained Shi Ye, there was light ahead and glory behind—is that not *xian hou*?”

sentences.zh: 自吾得由也，惡言不至門，是非禦侮邪？』」

translations.literal: Since I obtained You, abusive words do not reach my gate—is this not “warding off insult”?””
translations.idiomatic: “Once I gained Zhong You, slander never crossed my threshold—is that not *yu wu*?””

sentences.zh: 若，汝也。

translations.literal: Ruo means “you” (ru).
translations.idiomatic: Here *ruo* is the pronoun “you.”

sentences.zh: 皆為文簿而上之。

translations.literal: In every case they made written registers and submitted them upward.
translations.idiomatic: Each office drew up written returns and forwarded them to the throne.

sentences.zh: 條侯，周亞夫也。

translations.literal: The Marquis of Tiao was Zhou Yafu.
translations.idiomatic: The “Tiao marquis” is Zhou Yafu of the Western Han.

sentences.zh: 為將軍，軍於細柳，文帝幸其營，亞夫持兵揖曰：「介冑之士不拜，請以軍禮見。

translations.literal: Serving as general, he encamped at Xiliu; when Emperor Wen visited his camp, Yafu, weapons in hand, bowed with folded hands and said: “A man in armor does not perform the full prostration—allow me to meet you with military courtesy.
translations.idiomatic: As general at the Xiliu camp, Zhou Yafu met Emperor Wen in full harness, saluted with arms cradled, and said: “A mailed officer cannot kowtow; I beg leave to greet Your Majesty by the rules of the camp.”

sentences.zh: 」文帝曰：「此真將軍也！

translations.literal: ” Emperor Wen said: “This is a true general!”
translations.idiomatic: Wen exclaimed: “There is the real article of a field commander!”

sentences.zh: 」穰苴，齊人田穰苴也。

translations.literal: ” Rangju was the Qi man Tian Rangju.
translations.idiomatic: Tian Rangju of Qi is the strategist meant by *Rangju*.

sentences.zh: 齊景公使為將軍，使莊賈往，穰苴與約曰：「旦日日中會於軍門。

translations.literal: Duke Jing of Qi made him general and sent Zhuang Jia; Rangju made an appointment, saying: “Tomorrow at noon we meet at the army gate.
translations.idiomatic: Qi’s Duke Jing commissioned him and paired him with Zhuang Jia; Rangju set the rendezvous: “Tomorrow at noon outside the camp gate.”

sentences.zh: 」穰苴先至，賈後至，於是遂斬莊賈以徇三軍，士皆振栗。

translations.literal: ” Rangju arrived first; Jia arrived late, whereupon he beheaded Zhuang Jia before the three armies, and the soldiers all trembled.
translations.idiomatic: Rangju was punctual, Zhuang Jia was late, so Rangju executed Jia before the host to establish discipline, and every man shook with awe.

sentences.zh: 獷音古猛反，又音久永反。

translations.literal: Guang is read gu-meng fanqie, also jiu-yong fanqie.
translations.idiomatic: The commentary gives two fanqie readings for *guang*: gu-meng and jiu-yong.

sentences.zh: 徼人謂徼外人偏何等也。

translations.literal: “Frontier people” means people beyond the pale such as Pian He.
translations.idiomatic: “Men beyond the frontier” refers to leaders like the Wuhuan chief Pian He.

sentences.zh: 符，驗也。

translations.literal: A tally: proof.
translations.idiomatic: Here *fu* means a token of verification.

sentences.zh: 為偏何請還自□，以驗內屬之信。

translations.literal: He asked on Pian He’s behalf that he return in person to the capital as proof of good faith in submitting.
translations.idiomatic: The court had Pian He go back in person to the capital so his allegiance could be tested and trusted.

sentences.zh: 數級謂偏何斬匈奴，送首級受賞賜。

translations.literal: “Counting severed heads” means Pian He beheaded Xiongnu, sent in heads, and received rewards.
translations.idiomatic: “Tallying heads” describes how Pian He cut down Xiongnu, forwarded the skulls, and collected imperial bounty.

sentences.zh: 三十年為一世，言承化久也。

translations.literal: Thirty years make one generation; it means long-enduring transformation.
translations.idiomatic: Thirty years count as a generation—here the point is how slowly kingly virtue reshapes the realm.

sentences.zh: 論語孔子曰：「如有王者，必世而後仁。」

translations.literal: The *Analects* records Confucius: “If there were a true king, surely after a generation there would be benevolence.”
translations.idiomatic: Confucius says that if a perfect sovereign arose, humaneness would need a full generation to take root.

sentences.zh: 眚，過也。

translations.literal: Sheng: fault.
translations.idiomatic: Here *sheng* means a blemish or slip.

sentences.zh: 左傳曰：「不以一眚掩大德。

translations.literal: The *Zuo Tradition* says: “Do not let one fault mask great virtue.
translations.idiomatic: The *Zuo zhuan* warns against letting a single misstep eclipse great merit.

sentences.zh: 」眚音所景反。

translations.literal: ” Sheng is read suo-jing fanqie.
translations.idiomatic: The commentary glosses *sheng* with the suo-jing fanqie.

sentences.zh: 畏法猶嚴法也。

translations.literal: “Fearing the law” is the same as “strict law.”
translations.idiomatic: “Stand in awe of the law” is equivalent here to “submit to its severity.”
